lesson.menu_title: Negotiating & Reaching Agreement -- Getting to Yes
lesson.menu_desc: Conditional conjunctions (unless / as long as / provided that) -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Negotiating & Reaching Agreement
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 19 -- Negotiating & Reaching Agreement

listenings.text: A good negotiation is not a fight; it is a search for common ground. Last month I had to renew the contract with our main cloud vendor, and the first offer was simply too high. So I prepared carefully. I listed everything we wanted, and I separated the deal-breakers from the small concessions. My biggest leverage was our two-year commitment, because volume matters to any vendor. When we met, I did not start with the price. I started with the relationship, and I made it clear that I wanted a win-win, not a win for one side. Then I set my conditions. I told them we could sign a longer contract, but only as long as they improved the response time. I said we would commit to more volume provided that the unit price dropped. And I made one thing very clear: unless they guaranteed ninety-nine point nine percent uptime, we could not agree at all. In the end, we both made a concession. They lowered the price, and we extended the term. We did not just settle for less; we found a deal that was good for both companies, and the relationship is stronger than before. That is the goal: get to yes without losing the partner.

Hi Rafael, this is Sofia from procurement. Great news about the cloud contract. The finance team reviewed the final terms, and they were impressed. They told me you saved almost twenty percent without damaging the relationship with the vendor, and that is exactly what we needed. One person on the board said it was the best negotiation result of the year. So here is a new request. The leadership team would like you to lead the negotiation for our new software licenses next quarter. They said the budget is tighter this time, so we will need every bit of your leverage. One condition from finance, though: we can approve the project as long as the total stays under the cap, and we cannot sign anything unless there is a clear exit clause. Could you put together a short negotiation plan, with your deal-breakers and your possible concessions? They said it should be ready before the next steering meeting. Thank you, Rafael. You really know how to find common ground. Talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, I listed what we wanted and separated the deal-breakers from the small concessions. Next, I identified my main leverage, our two-year commitment. Then I opened with the relationship and made it clear I wanted a win-win. After that, I set my conditions using if, unless, and as long as. Finally, we both made a concession and found common ground, so we reached an agreement that was good for both sides.
